title: Riven hazel mallet
subtitle: A one-piece carving mallet from a hazel trunk-branch junction
excerpt: A carving mallet does not need to be turned on a lathe. A section of hazel trunk where a large branch meets the main stem provides a naturally bulbous head with a straight handle extension in one piece of wood. The grain runs continuously through both parts; the mallet is as strong as any turned equivalent. Rive the section from the tree, axe it to shape, and put it to immediate use.
sourceNotes: Integral-handle wooden mallets from tree junctions documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and in British rural craft traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled wood still high in moisture content. Green hazel is soft enough to shape quickly with axe and drawknife; the mallet is shaped green and hardens as it seasons on the bench.

Split along the grain using an axe or wedge. Riving the mallet section from the hazel trunk ensures the grain runs continuously through both head and handle, which is what gives the piece its strength.
A short section of timber prepared for working. The mallet billet is the trunk-branch junction section, including the bulge of the junction and a straight handle length.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body to shave material from green wood. Used here on the shaving horse to bring the handle to round section.

projectSchedule.title: Shape and balance the mallet
projectSchedule.body: Axe the head to shape, drawknife the handle, check balance. The mallet can be used immediately.

projectSchedule.title: Mallet seasoned: fully hardened
projectSchedule.body: A month of bench use or storage seasons the green hazel. The mallet is fully hardened and will not shrink further.

content.text: Most carving mallets are turned on a lathe from a single round billet. This mallet is not: it comes from the natural junction where a branch meets the main stem of a hazel trunk, a point where the wood is already wider than the rest of the stem and where the grain runs continuously through both what will become the head and the handle. A
, produces a serviceable carving mallet in under two hours.

that includes a trunk-branch junction, with the trunk section providing at least 25 cm of handle length and the junction bulge at least 9 to 10 cm across. This kind of section comes from a coppice stool where a large multi-stem branch meets the main trunk, or from a junction pruned from a mature hazel. Ash and apple at equivalent junctions are good alternatives; apple junction wood is very dense and makes a particularly durable mallet head.

A carving axe for roughing the head shape. A drawknife and shaving horse for bringing the handle to round section. A sloyd knife for any fine work at the junction. A leather strop. Light sandpaper (180 grit) for the handle is optional; a drawknife finish is adequate for a mallet handle.
Step 1: Identify and mark the mallet form

Look at the junction billet from several angles. The head should be the widest part of the junction, the handle the straight stem below it. Mark the intended head cylinder with pencil: a cylinder about 8 to 10 cm in diameter and 8 cm long. The handle extends from the base of this cylinder down the trunk section, tapering slightly toward the grip end. A mallet handle of 22 to 25 cm is the right length for bench carving.
Step 2: Axe the head to rough cylinder

The junction area will have irregular protrusions from the branch attachment. Remove these with the axe, working in a chop-and-rotate pattern around the head circumference. The goal is a roughly cylindrical head with the face ends flat and roughly perpendicular to the handle axis. Work carefully near the handle junction: too heavy an axe stroke here removes material from the neck where the head meets the handle, which weakens it.
Clamp the handle section on the shaving horse with the head overhanging the far end of the platform. Use the

to bring the handle to an oval section, working from the head junction toward the grip end. Rotate the work in the clamp after each pass to work all faces. The handle should taper from about 28 mm at the head junction to 22 mm at the grip. Green hazel shaves quickly; check diameter often.

The two striking faces of the mallet head should be roughly flat and parallel. Check with a straight edge laid across each face. Any high spots can be removed with the axe in light chop-and-rock cuts, or with the sloyd knife if the material to remove is small. The faces need not be machine-flat, but a pronounced hollow or dome will cause the mallet to bounce unpredictably on a chisel handle.

Hold the mallet by the grip end and swing it lightly. A well-balanced mallet feels as if it wants to return to centre after each blow; an unbalanced one feels as if it wants to roll. If the mallet consistently rolls in one direction, the head has more material on that side. Remove the excess with the axe. This is a matter of feel; there is no precise measure.

A carving mallet receives no food-safe finish. Linseed oil can be applied to the handle for comfort but is not necessary. The mallet is ready to use immediately. As it seasons on the bench over the next few weeks, the green wood will harden and the handle will shrink slightly to a firmer grip.
Make the head larger (12 cm diameter) from an ash trunk-branch junction for a heavier mallet used in green-woodwork joinery rather than bench carving.
Carve a chamfer around the striking-face edges of the head to reduce the risk of the head splitting at the rim on an off-centre blow.

Store on the bench or hung on a tool wall out of direct sunlight. Inspect the neck annually for any developing crack at the head-handle junction; a crack here must be addressed before the head loosens.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when shaping and using the mallet.

items.symptom: The head is loose on the handle after seasoning
items.cause: The green wood at the neck shrank during drying, creating play between the head and the handle section.
items.fix: This should not happen if the head and handle are continuous grain from a single piece: they will shrink together. If they do loosen, the billet was not truly a single piece of grain. Drive a thin hardwood wedge into a saw cut at the handle end and tap it into the head bore to tighten.

items.symptom: The handle cracks along the grain during drying
items.cause: Rapid drying or a node in the handle section where grain deviation causes differential shrinkage.
items.fix: Hazel handles with a node sometimes split; store away from heat and sun. A small crack along the handle does not compromise structural integrity if it runs with the grain. Wipe with linseed oil and continue using.

items.symptom: The mallet head feels too light for the work
items.cause: The head cylinder is too small in diameter or the species is naturally light.
items.fix: For future work, select a junction section with a wider head diameter, or use apple or ash which are denser than hazel.
